articles.title: Getting Started
articles.body:
# Welcome to CODEX

CODEX is a **distraction-respectful Bible study environment** that fuses classical scripture with modern AI lenses. It runs entirely in your browser as a PWA — install it to your home screen for an app-like experience.

## The shape of the app

- On a desktop, CODEX is **The Desk**: the Word in the center, and every instrument — Oracle, Galaxy, Thread Web, Atlas, River, Shelves — opens as **its own window** you can drag, snap, and dock.
- On a phone, CODEX is **The Palm**: the Word edge to edge, one floating **orb**, and a glass sheet that is the single door to everything.
- Everywhere, **⌘K** (or tapping ⌘ in the trace, top-right) opens the **omnibar** — type a reference, a panel name, or a question, and go.

## Your first five minutes

1. Read. Arrow keys walk verses (↓/↑) and chapters (→/←).
2. **Click any verse** — the verse menu offers Cross-Refs, Strong's, Word Study, the Atlas, marks, and more.
3. Press **⌘K** and type `galaxy` — the whole canon as one navigable sky.
4. Press **O** — the Oracle, a careful scholar that cites chapter and verse.
5. Press **?** — every keyboard shortcut on one card.

## Settings & help

Open Settings from the omnibar (type `settings`) or the dock. The Settings window is searchable — type "font", "dark", or "api key" and the matching controls surface live. The HELP tab at its top is this manual.

> CODEX is offline-first: scripture, your marks, and your notes live on your device. AI features are optional and require an API key (Settings → AI & KEYS).

articles.title: The Desk
articles.body:
# The Desk

On a desktop, CODEX is a desk: the Word in the center, instruments as **windows**. Since v11 (*sola fenestra*) every panel is its own window — the old study stack is gone.

## Windows

- Open any panel from **⌘K**, the dock, or keys **1–9** — it arrives as a window with a glyph, a title, and the current reference in its header.
- **Drag** windows anywhere; **snap** them to screen edges; **dock** them to a chip and recall them later.
- Geometry persists per window — your desk is laid out the way you left it, across reloads.

## Focus

Press **F** (or the ⛶ button in any window header) and everything but the reader fades — the Word alone. Press F or Esc to return.

## The quiet chrome

There is no status bar. What remains is **the trace** — time · theme · ⌘ — top-right, nearly transparent until you reach for it. Everything else lives in the dock and the omnibar.

## One door

⌘K opens the omnibar: type a reference ("john 3:16"), a panel name ("galaxy", "river", "atlas"), a command, or a question. The omnibar learns what you use most and surfaces it first.

articles.title: The Palm — CODEX on the Phone
articles.body:
# The Palm

On a phone the app is **the Word plus exactly one piece of chrome: the orb**. No bars, no drawers.

## The orb

One floating glass orb rides the bottom edge. Drag it to the left, center, or right third — it remembers. It **pulses while anything loads**, and a **gold arc** burns around it while your reading streak is alive.

- **Tap** the orb → the Palm opens.
- **Long-press** the orb → focus: even the trace dies; only the Word and the orb remain.

## The Palm — the one door

A glass bottom sheet with three zones:

1. **GRIP** — ⌘ omnibar · ☰ shelves · ◬ oracle · ✦ marks · ❂ galaxy, plus your most-used panel as a learned chip.
2. **VERBS** — actions bound to the current verse (⚔ ⌬ ◎) and ⟳ CONTINUE to resume where you were.
3. **PANELS** — every panel, builtin and plugin; each opens as a **full-screen sheet** hosting the same component the desk windows host.

## Sheets

At most two sheets stack. **Swipe down** the header to dismiss, **swipe in from the left edge** (or ‹) to go back, Esc works with a keyboard. Navigating scripture closes everything — the Palm is a door, not a place.

articles.title: The Trace
articles.body:
# The Trace

CODEX has no status bar. What remains is the **trace**: a near-transparent strip, top-right, that you only notice when you reach for it.

## On the desk

- **The time** — hover for the full date.
- **◐ / ◑** — one tap toggles light/dark (with auto-theme, the tooltip says so).
- **⌘** — opens the omnibar (same as ⌘K).

## On the palm

The phone's trace is even quieter: time · ◐, top-right over the Word. Long-press the orb and even the trace dies — only scripture remains.

Everything the old status bar carried now lives in the dock and the omnibar. The trace is deliberate: chrome should be *reachable*, never *present*.

articles.title: Boot Intro Animation
articles.body:
# The Boot Intro

On first launch CODEX runs a brief **terminal-style boot sequence** — scanlines, ASCII glyphs, an opening verse, and the CODEX wordmark resolving in. It's intentionally short (~2 seconds) and only plays once per session.

## Skipping

- Tap anywhere or press any key to skip immediately.
- Press `Esc` during the sequence to dismiss the splash overlay.

## Disabling permanently

Open **Settings → Look → Boot intro** and switch it off. The next launch will jump straight to the reader. You can re-enable it any time — useful for the dramatic effect when demoing CODEX to someone new.

## Why it exists

The boot is part of CODEX's sci-fi terminal aesthetic. It also gives the service worker a moment to hydrate the offline shell before the reader paints.

articles.title: Reading Controls
articles.body:
# Reading Controls

All reading controls live in **Settings → Reading**.

## Typography

- **Font size** — slider, 12–28 px. Persists per device.
- **Font face** — choose between a serif (Cormorant Garamond), a sans (Inter Tight), or mono (JetBrains Mono).
- **Line height & measure** — auto-tuned per font; tweak in Look.

## Overlays

- **Red letter** — words attributed to Jesus rendered in the accent warn color. Sourced from `data/red-letter.json`.
- **YHWH restoration** — replaces *LORD* in OT passages with the Tetragrammaton (יהוה) where the underlying Hebrew has it.
- **Verse numbers** — show, hide, or render them as faint marginalia.

## Side-by-side

Toggle **Side-by-side** (or press `S`) to render two translations in parallel columns — handy for Greek/English or KJV/modern comparison.

articles.title: Font Size & Face
articles.body:
# Font Controls

CODEX gives you fine-grained control over how scripture looks on the page.

## Size cycle (Aa)

The **Aa** button in the reading toolbar cycles through five size presets: XS, S, M, L, XL. Tap repeatedly to step through; long-press to jump straight to M. The size persists per device and survives reloads.

## Face

- **Serif** (Cormorant Garamond) — the default, optimized for long-form reading.
- **Sans** (Inter Tight) — clean and modern, good for shorter sessions or screens with low DPI.
- **Mono** (JetBrains Mono) — for the terminal-aesthetic experience or for studying poetry where alignment matters.

Switch in **Settings → Look → Scripture face**. The change is instant — no remount.

## Hebrew & Greek

When viewing Hebrew or Greek translations CODEX automatically swaps to a script-appropriate fallback that handles vowel pointing and diacritics correctly.

articles.title: Theme Modes (Light / Dark / Auto)
articles.body:
# Theme Modes

CODEX supports three theme modes selectable in **Settings → Look → Theme**:

- **Light** — high-contrast paper-like background, good in daylight.
- **Dark** — the default, with a deep navy/black canvas and cyan HUD accents. Easy on the eyes for evening study.
- **Auto** — solar-aware. CODEX computes the approximate sun position from your local time and slides between light and dark automatically across dawn and dusk.

## Accent color

In Look you can also pick a **HUD accent** — cyan, amber, magenta, or green — which tints buttons, focus rings, and the sci-fi chrome.

## Scanlines

A subtle scanline overlay is on by default for the terminal aesthetic. Toggle it off if it bothers your eyes.

All three settings persist via `localStorage` and sync across devices when you enable cross-device sync.

articles.title: Red Letter Mode
articles.body:
# Red Letter Mode

When enabled, every word attributed to Jesus Christ is rendered in the accent warn color (a soft red in light mode, a warm coral in dark). The data comes from `data/red-letter.json`, a per-verse map of speaker ranges.

## Coverage

- All four Gospels.
- Acts 9 (Damascus road) and Acts 22 / 26 (Paul's retellings).
- Revelation 1–3 (the letters to the seven churches) and Rev 22:7–20.

## Enabling

**Settings → Reading → Red letter**. The change is instant and per-translation — works against any installed English or original-language translation that follows the canonical verse numbering.

## Edge cases

- Embedded quotations within Jesus's speech (e.g. Jesus quoting Isaiah) stay red.
- Indirect speech ("he said that…") stays in normal color.
- For Hebrew/Greek translations the marks fall back to the matched English range.

articles.title: YHWH Restoration
articles.body:
# YHWH Restoration

In most English Bibles, the divine name **יהוה** (the Tetragrammaton) is rendered as *LORD* in small caps. YHWH Restoration substitutes the actual four-letter name back into the text wherever the underlying Hebrew has it.

## Enabling

**Settings → Reading → YHWH restoration**. Off by default.

## How it works

CODEX cross-references the verse against the Westminster Leningrad Codex to find positions where the consonantal text reads יהוה. At those positions it replaces the English *LORD* with יהוה (or with the romanization *Yahweh* if you prefer — see the sub-toggle).

## What it does not touch

- *Lord* in lowercase (which represents *Adonai* or the Greek κύριος).
- *GOD* in small caps (typically *Adonai YHWH* — handled by a separate sub-toggle).
- New Testament references — Greek manuscripts use κύριος; CODEX does not retroject.

articles.title: Side-by-Side Translations
articles.body:
# Side-by-Side Mode

Read two translations in parallel columns — invaluable for original-language study or for cross-checking renderings.

## Enabling

Press `S` or open **Settings → Reading → Side-by-side**. A chip strip appears beneath the passage; tap a chip to set the second column's translation.

## How it lays out

- Verses align by reference, not by line — long verses in one translation expand both rows so nothing drifts.
- On phones, the second column drops below each verse instead of beside it (responsive stacking).
- Both translations honor your red-letter and YHWH settings.

## Use cases

- KJV on the left, NIV on the right for sense-check.
- WLC (Hebrew) on the left, KJV on the right for OT study.
- Tisch (Greek) + your working English for NT exegesis.

articles.title: Navigation
articles.body:
# Navigation

## Book picker

Tap the reference (e.g. *John 1:1*) in the top bar to open the **book picker** — three-pane: testament, book, chapter. Type to filter.

## Chapter swipe

On touch devices, swipe left/right on the passage to advance/rewind a chapter.

## Keyboard

- `J` / `K` — next / previous verse (vim-style)
- `H` / `L` — previous / next chapter (rolls into the next book at the edges)
- `Cmd/Ctrl+K` — focus search
- `Enter` on a focused verse — opens the verse menu
- `Esc` — close popovers / overlays

See the **Keyboard Shortcuts** article for the full set.

articles.title: Translations — Lanes & Offline Dots
articles.body:
# Translations

Press **T** (or ⌘K → `translations`) to open the Tongues instrument.

## The current Word

The top block shows the active verse in your **primary translation** — a serene serif block fronted by the translation's identity. One glance answers "what am I reading".

## Language lanes

Translations are grouped in horizontal **lanes by tongue** — English, Español, Français, Deutsch, Português, Latina, עברית, Ἑλληνική, हिन्दी. Each translation is a **card** (glyph · year · license dot), not a text row.

- **One tap** on a card makes it your primary translation — the reader follows instantly.
- **⊕** on a card's corner (desktop) or **long-press** (touch) toggles it into the **compare set**; compare renderings stack beneath the primary.
- ←/→ walk the cards of a lane from the keyboard.

## Offline dots — the truth, permanently visible

Every card carries its offline-state dot at all times:

- **●** fully bundled or cached — reads with no network.
- **◐** partly cached — some chapters live on your device.
- **○** network — fetched as you read.

**The dot is the control**: click it to download a translation for offline use, pause a download, or clear the cache. No separate downloads screen to hunt for.

articles.title: Apocrypha & Extra-Canonical Books
articles.body:
# Apocrypha & Extra-Canonical

CODEX treats canon as a setting, not a fact. Several extra-canonical corpora are available:

## Deuterocanon (Catholic / Orthodox)

Tobit, Judith, Wisdom, Sirach, Baruch, 1–2 Maccabees, plus the Greek additions to Esther and Daniel. Install the Catholic or Orthodox translations to surface them in the book picker.

## Ethiopian canon

Includes **1 Enoch**, **Jubilees**, and **2 Esdras** alongside the broader Old and New Testaments. Install the Ethiopian translation.

## Nag Hammadi Library

Gospel of Thomas, Gospel of Philip, Gospel of Truth, Apocryphon of John, and others. Install as a separate corpus; appears under **Library → Gnostic** in the book picker.

## Filtering

**Settings → Reading → Canon filter** lets you hide or show extra-canonical books per your tradition. The Gnosis panel makes heavy use of these corpora when enabled.

articles.title: The Shelves — Every Canon, Honestly
articles.body:
# The Shelves

⌘K → `library` (or ☰ in the Palm) opens the Shelves — the whole library, every canon, all the time.

## Every shelf, always visible

The Old Testament, the New Testament, **Apocrypha · Deuterocanon, Orthodox, Ethiopian (Ge'ez), Armenian, Syriac · Peshitta, Coptic additions, and Pseudepigrapha** — no shelf is hidden just because your current translation doesn't carry it.

## Source lights — the Lost Books honesty

Every book carries a **source light**, and the light never lies:

- **● cyan** — your current translation has this book; tap and read.
- **◐ violet** — another corpus in the registry carries it; the reader auto-serves from there.
- **○ dim** — **no known source yet**. The book exists in a canon list, but CODEX has no text for it and says so plainly, rather than pretending.

This is deliberate: many so-called *lost books* survive only as names or fragments. A shelf that shows ○ honestly is worth more than one that hides the gap.

## Using the shelves

Click a book → its chapter grid unfolds in place. Click a chapter → the reader jumps (switching corpus automatically if needed). One search line covers book names, references ("john 3:16"), and full text.

articles.title: Verse Menu
articles.body:
# Verse Menu

Tap any verse to anchor the **Verse Menu** beside it. Every per-verse action lives here so the reader stays uncluttered.

## Actions

- **✦ MARK** — add to bookmarks with optional color and note.
- **Α/Ω COMPARE** — open verse-compare across all installed translations.
- **↔ TRANSLATE** — quick switch the primary translation just for this passage.
- **◉ ASK ORACLE** — prefill Oracle with the verse and a starter prompt.
- **⎘ COPY** — copy text to clipboard with reference attached.
- **🌐 MAP** — open the verse map.
- **🎨 ART** — open verse art.
- **⟁ GNOSIS** — engage the gnosis overlay.
- **⚔ QUEST** — add to an active reading quest.

Plugins can add their own rows via the `verseActions` hook (see the **Plugins** article).

Keyboard: focus a verse with `J`/`K` and press `Enter` to open the menu.

articles.title: The Atlas — the Cinematic Map
articles.body:
# The Atlas

Press **M** on any verse (or choose **Map** from the verse menu) to open the Atlas — a cinematic, era-aware map of the biblical world.

## The camera flies

When the reader moves, the **camera flies** to the new verse's setting — a smooth cinematic approach (a clean cut under reduced-motion). The verse's primary place pulses; points of interest from the passage surround it.

## The time scrub

Drag the **time scrub** at the foot of the map and watch **empires recolor the land** — polities rise and fall under your finger, and places appear or vanish according to when they were known by that name (Constantinople and Istanbul are different dots in different centuries).

## Place dossiers

**Click any place** for its dossier: era, century, period context, populations and structures, scripture references that touch it (served from a local gazetteer first — instant, offline, zero tokens — then AI if needed), and a Wikipedia preview when available.

A quiet orb shows when the Atlas is thinking; an accessible list of real buttons mirrors every point on the map.

## Honesty

Coordinates and dates are scholarly approximations. Where the AI fills a profile, it is cached locally (`codex.maps.*`) and marked as generated — the Atlas never passes generation off as archaeology.

articles.title: Verse Art
articles.body:
# Verse Art

**Verse Art** surfaces 6–8 notable artworks depicting the scene of any verse — paintings, illuminated manuscripts, frescoes, mosaics.

## Opening

From the verse menu, tap **🎨 ART**. The first open for a verse calls the AI (a fast, low-cost tier) for the curated list; results are cached forever in `localStorage` under `codex.art.${book}.${ch}.${verse}` so re-opens are instant and offline-safe.

## What you get

A grid of cards, each with:

- A thumbnail from **Wikimedia Commons** (resolved through `Special:FilePath`) when available, otherwise a stylised placeholder.
- Title, artist, year, medium, and the holding museum.
- A short note explaining the connection to the verse.

## More

The **+ MORE** button issues a follow-up call passing the titles already shown, so the AI returns a different set rather than repeating itself. Stack as many rounds as you like — each is cached.

## Limitations

Thumbnails depend on Wikimedia hosting the file under the exact name the model guessed. When that fails, the card still shows the metadata.

articles.title: Verse Mirror
articles.body:
# Verse Mirror

**Verse Mirror** surfaces historical, geopolitical, and prophetic resonances for any verse. Open it from the verse menu.

## Four sections

- **Historical parallels** — 3–6 events through history that mirror the passage's theme, with date, place, and a brief connection note.
- **Modern resonances** — recent (~30 years) geopolitical events where scholars or commentators have drawn parallels to the verse.
- **Prophetic readings** — eschatological interpretations from **multiple traditions** (premillennial, amillennial, preterist, historicist, idealist). The Mirror never endorses one school — each block is labelled by tradition so you see the spread.
- **Cross-references** — clickable canonical refs that jump in-app.

## Tone & caveats

Every AI-generated block is flagged as a **scholarly survey, not prediction**. The prompt explicitly forbids single-tradition prophecy claims. Use Mirror as a starting point for your own reading, not as the final word.

Results cache under `codex.mirrors.${book}.${ch}.${verse}` and persist offline.

articles.title: Verse Compare (Deep)
articles.body:
# Verse Compare

**Verse Compare** displays a single verse side-by-side across every translation you have installed. Reach it from the verse menu's **COMPARE** action or from the bottom toolbar of the compare modal.

## Modal layout

- One row per translation, sorted alphabetically (pin favorites to the top in settings).
- A dropdown at the top filters by language, era, or tradition (Hebrew, Greek, Latin, English Modern, English Historic, etc.).
- A bottom toolbar lets you add adjacent verses (-1 / +1) for context — the modal grows a row for each new verse, keeping all translations aligned by reference.

## Loading & caching

Missing chapters are fetched on demand via `BIBLE.loadChapter` and cached, so reopening the same comparison is instant and works offline.

## Workflow tips

- Open a contested verse (e.g. Romans 5:12, Isaiah 7:14) and add `±2` for the full sentence shape.
- Drag any row to copy a SBL-formatted citation with translation, edition, and access date.

articles.title: Reading Quests
articles.body:
# Reading Quests

Quests are **threaded reading plans** — sequences of verses unified by a theme. As you read, the quest tracks your progress and unlocks the next stop.

## Available quests

- **Messiah quest** — every messianic prophecy in the Hebrew Bible and its NT fulfillment, in canonical order.
- **Wisdom quest** — Proverbs, Job, Ecclesiastes, and Sirach woven into a single arc.
- **Apocalypse quest** — Daniel, Ezekiel 1, Zechariah, Revelation, parts of 1 Enoch.

## How it works

Open the verse menu → **Add to Quest**. Or start a quest from Settings → Quests. Progress shows as a thin progress bar above the passage. Completed quests stay in your library for review.

Quests are local; they sync via the cross-device sync key.

articles.title: Messiah in Prophecy Quest
articles.body:
# Messiah in Prophecy

A full-screen guided study tour covering **50 core messianic prophecies** from the Hebrew Bible and their New Testament fulfillments.

## Launching

Tap the **⚔ QUESTS** button in the status bar, then choose *Messiah in Prophecy*. The reader fades and the quest overlay mounts in its place.

## Layout

- **Sidebar** — all 50 cards. Completed ones show a check; current is highlighted.
- **Main panel** — the prophecy text, the fulfillment text, and a short scholarly note.
- **Debate Ready panel** — common objections (Jewish, skeptical, alternative-fulfillment) and how Christian apologists respond, presented as a survey not a polemic.
- **Sticky bottom bar** — previous / next, pin to passage, exit.

## Progress

Your position persists to `localStorage` under `codex.quest.messiah.*` so you can resume across sessions. The quest can be reset from Settings → Quests.

## Future quests

Plugins can register additional quests via `window.CODEX_QUESTS` — see the **Plugins** article.

articles.title: AI Study Panels
articles.body:
# AI Study Panels

CODEX ships five AI-generated study panels — each opens as its own window on the desk, or a full-screen sheet on the phone (⌘K, the dock, or keys 1–9). Each is computed per-verse and cached locally so the same passage never costs twice.

## Talmud

Rabbinic commentary lens: tractate refs, halakhic vs aggadic notes, midrash parallels.

## Commentary

A balanced verse-by-verse commentary drawing on patristic, reformation, and modern sources. Default lens for most readers.

## Gematria · Intelligent Cross-Referencing

Not just a calculator. The panel computes **eleven Hebrew systems** (Hechrachi, Gadol, Sidduri, Katan, Katan Mispari, Bone'eh, Kidmi, Atbash, Albam, Ne'elam, Ha'akhor), plus Greek isopsephy and three English systems. The AI picks a primary word, names its symbolic meaning, and surfaces **every verse with the same value** — from canonical tradition AND from your own cached library. Click any cross-match to jump. See *Gematria · Deep*.

## Gnosis

Gnostic / esoteric / mystical readings. Off by default; enable in Settings → AI. Includes Nag Hammadi, Hermetica, and Kabbalistic cross-references.

## Apologetics

Classical defenses, common objections, manuscript notes. Useful for evangelism and skeptical inquiry.

Switch panels with number keys `1`–`9`.

articles.title: Gematria · Deep
articles.body:
# Gematria · Intelligent Cross-Referencing

When you open the **Σn** panel CODEX runs three layers in parallel:

1. **An offline compute library** (`gematria.js`) that calculates every major numerological system for the primary word.
2. **An AI synthesis** that picks the primary word, names its symbolic meaning, and lists canonical cross-matches (e.g. נחש *nachash* = 358 = משיח *Mashiach*).
3. **A live in-browser index** of every verse you have cached. Same-value matches surface instantly — your reading history becomes a personal concordance.

## The systems

**Hebrew:** Mispar Hechrachi (standard), Gadol (finals 500–900), Sidduri (ordinal 1–22), Katan (each letter reduced then summed), Katan Mispari (digital root), Bone'eh (cumulative), Kidmi (triangular), Atbash (א↔ת cipher), Albam (split-and-swap), Ne'elam (spelled-out name minus letter), Ha'akhor (value × position).

**Greek:** classical isopsephy (α=1 … ω=800), ordinal (1–24), reduced.

**English:** ordinal (a=1 … z=26), reduction (1–9), reverse.

## Cross-matches

Every row shows `[ref] [word] — note`. Click to jump. Two tabs: **FROM CANON** (AI's training) and **FROM YOUR LIBRARY** (computed locally from cached verses — grows as you read).

## Why numerology matters

In the rabbinic tradition gematria is a mnemonic and homiletic device used by the Baal HaTurim, the Zohar, and midrashists to surface conceptual links between passages. Christian numerologists (Augustine, the Greek Fathers, Reuchlin) used isopsephy similarly. Treat speculative correspondences as study aids, not proofs.

## Spot-check values

אהבה = 13, אחד = 13, חי = 18, משיח = 358, נחש = 358, יהוה = 26, λόγος = 373, Ἰησοῦς = 888.

articles.title: The Oracle & Its Powers
articles.body:
# The Oracle & Its Powers

Press **O** (or ⌘K → `oracle`) to open the Oracle — a scholarly companion bound to what you are reading. On the desk it is its own window; on the phone, a full-screen sheet.

## Rich, living answers

Every reply renders as a real document, not a wall of text:

- Every scripture reference becomes a **live chip** — hover to preview, click and the reader goes there. The chips exist so you can *check the Oracle's work*.
- The Oracle can emit **working buttons**, **charts**, **flowcharts**, and **verse grids** inline when they serve the answer.
- Words of Jesus render red-letter, exactly as in the reader.

## Threads as tabs

Conversations persist as **tabs** across sessions (up to 12 threads). Your earlier single-thread history migrates automatically into the first tab.

## ⚒ TOOLS — the Oracle drives the app

With the **⚒ TOOLS** toggle on, the Oracle may call the app's own tools mid-answer: read a passage, run a search, pull cross-references, open a panel, jump the reader, change a setting. Two honesty rules are load-bearing:

- **Every action is shown** as a chip in the thread — a settings write echoes a visible "⚙ set …" chip. Nothing is ever silent.
- Tool results are computed **locally by the app** — the Oracle never fabricates a search hit.

## Honesty rules

The Oracle cites chapter and verse for every claim that has one, never invents quotations, labels genuinely disputed readings **CONTESTED**, and gives Hebrew/Greek in its own script with transliteration and gloss.

A quiet orb pulses bottom-right whenever any AI call is in flight — you always know.

articles.title: Choosing an AI Provider
articles.body:
# Multi-Provider AI

CODEX can route Oracle and AI-panel calls through several providers. Switch in **Settings → AI → Provider**.

## Bring your own provider

Supply an API key for whichever provider you choose. Best quality for theological nuance, multi-tradition framing, and original-language analysis. Calls go through `/api/chat`, which proxies to your chosen provider. You can supply your own key in Settings → AI → Keys (synced to the server via `/api/key`).

## xAI Grok

Fast and inexpensive. Solid for cross-references and historical context; weaker on multi-tradition prophecy framing. Key stored locally only.

## Ollama (local)

For full offline AI: point CODEX at a local Ollama instance (`http://localhost:11434` by default). Quality depends entirely on the model you've pulled — Llama 3 70B is the sweet spot. Latency is local but generation can be slow on consumer hardware.

## Per-feature override

You can pin individual features (Oracle, Commentary, Gematria, Gnosis) to specific providers — useful if you want one provider for theology and another for quick lookups.

articles.title: Highlights, Bookmarks & Notes
articles.body:
# Highlights, Bookmarks & Notes

## Highlighting

Tap a verse → choose a color from the **mark strip**. The default color is set in Settings → Marks. Highlights sync across translations (the mark belongs to the reference, not the text).

## Bookmarks

In the verse menu, tap the **bookmark** icon. Bookmarks appear in the MARKS window (press `B`) with their reference, snippet, and any note. Drag to reorder, swipe to delete.

## Notes

Press `N` to open the notes drawer. Notes are markdown — `**bold**`, `*italic*`, lists, and `[[John 1:1]]` cross-reference links all work. Notes attach to a verse or float free.

## Where it lives

All marks, bookmarks, and notes are stored in `localStorage` under `codex.marks.*` keys, and ride your **cross-device sync** if enabled.

articles.title: Highlights & Colors (Deep)
articles.body:
# Highlights — Deep Dive

Highlights are reference-keyed, color-coded marks that ride with the verse no matter which translation you're reading.

## The mark strip

Tap a verse and the **mark strip** appears with eight color swatches (amber, rose, cyan, violet, green, slate, red, gold). Tap to apply; tap the same color again to remove. The default swatch is set in **Settings → Marks → Default color**.

## Custom palette

You can override any swatch in **Settings → Marks → Palette** — useful for color-coding your own taxonomy (e.g. cyan = covenant, violet = wisdom, gold = christological).

## Bulk operations

In **Settings → Marks → Manage** you can: filter by color, bulk re-color, bulk delete, and export selection to a markdown bundle. The whole mark set is part of cross-device sync.

## Across translations

Because a mark is stored as `{ ref, color }` rather than as text offsets, swapping translations preserves all your highlights — even into a Hebrew or Greek translation.

articles.title: Bookmarks (Deep)
articles.body:
# Bookmarks — Deep Dive

Bookmarks are the long-term version of highlights — saved references you want to find again, with optional notes and pin status.

## Creating

From the verse menu, tap the **✦ MARK** icon. A small bottom-sheet lets you pick a color, add a one-line note, and pin (pins float to the top of the bookmarks list).

## The marks window

Open with `B`. Each entry shows the reference, a short snippet of the verse, your note, and the color stripe. Drag to reorder; swipe left to delete; tap to jump to the passage.

## Search

The search box above the list does both substring and semantic search. If the substring pass returns nothing or thin results, **mark-search** (Oracle-powered, cached) re-ranks the full set by meaning — so typing *"love"* surfaces *1 Corinthians 13* even if you never wrote the word in your note.

## Storage

Bookmarks live in `localStorage` under `codex.marks.*` and ride cross-device sync.

articles.title: Notes System (Deep)
articles.body:
# Notes — Deep Dive

The Notes widget is a draggable, resizable, hidable pad designed for scholars who keep margin notes while reading.

## Opening & shaping

- Press `N` to toggle. Anchored bottom-right by default.
- A resize grip in the **top-left corner** lets you drag the panel to any width and height — the size persists in `codex.notes.size`.
- Drag the title bar to reposition; position also persists.

## Pin to verse

A note can be **pinned to the current verse**. Pinned notes auto-surface when you navigate back to that reference. Notes can also float free of any verse.

## Markdown

- Inline: `**bold**`, `*italic*`, `` `code` ``, `[text](url)`.
- Block: headings, ordered/unordered lists, blockquotes.
- Cross-references: `[[John 1:1]]` becomes a clickable jump.

## Where it lives

Every note is in `localStorage` so reloads are safe. The notes set rides the standard `codex.*` cross-device sync. Nothing leaves your device unless you opt into sync.

articles.title: Searching Marks Semantically
articles.body:
# Mark Search

The MARKS window has a small search box that does two passes:

1. **Literal substring** — searches reference strings, note text, and any cached snippet text. Instant, local.
2. **Semantic re-rank** — if the literal pass returns too few hits, CODEX asks Oracle to score every saved mark against your query and returns the top results, with a one-line *reason* explaining each match.

## Why it matters

Typing *"love"* surfaces *1 Corinthians 13* and *Romans 8:38* even when none of your marks have *love* in the reference or note.

## Caching

Results are cached in `localStorage["codex.marksearch.v1"]` keyed by a signature of the query plus the set of mark keys. Capped at ~50 entries (LRU). Identical queries against the same mark set are instant and free.

## Privacy

Only reference strings and your own notes are sent to the AI — never your full reading history or any other data.

articles.title: Offline Mode
articles.body:
# Offline Mode

CODEX is a true **Progressive Web App**. After your first visit, the entire reader works without network.

## What's cached automatically

- The app shell — every HTML, CSS, JS, JSX file.
- React, Babel, Leaflet (the JS framework deps).
- Any **scripture chapter** you read.
- Any **AI panel** you've opened (verse-keyed cache).
- Map tiles for chapters you've visited.

## What's NOT offline

- New Oracle questions (needs `/api/chat`).
- New AI panels for chapters you haven't visited.
- Installing a new translation.

## Install to home screen

On iOS: Share → Add to Home Screen. On Android/desktop Chrome: address-bar install icon. Once installed, CODEX launches as its own window.

articles.title: Auto-Cache (First-Install Warm-Up)
articles.body:
# Auto-Cache

For CODEX to truly be "install once, works forever" the user's primary translation needs every chapter on disk **before** they ever lose connectivity. The auto-cache module handles this.

## What it does

The first time you launch CODEX, it kicks off a background download of every chapter of your primary translation, then sets a flag in `localStorage["codex.autocache.v1"]` so it never runs again. Subsequent translations are still on-demand.

## Progress events

The footer shows a thin progress strip driven by these events:

- `codex:autocache-start { translation, total }`
- `codex:autocache-tick  { translation, done, total }`
- `codex:autocache-done  { translation, done, total }`
- `codex:autocache-error { translation, error }`

## Manual re-run

If you change primary translation later, trigger a fresh warm-up from **Settings → Bible → Download all**. You can also clear the autocache flag from Settings → Cache to redo the warm-up.

## Size budget

The KJV warm-up is ~5 MB. Pre-pulling every translation would be tens of MB — not the default.

articles.title: Cross-Device Sync
articles.body:
# Cross-Device Sync

CODEX supports two sync backends so your marks, notes, quests, and settings ride across phones, tablets, and desktops.

## Mode 1 — GitHub Gist (recommended)

One-step setup. Paste a GitHub Personal Access Token with `gist` scope. CODEX creates a private gist and uses it as the sync target. The "personal link" is the gist URL — open it on any device, paste the same token, and the app joins the sync.

## Mode 2 — Firebase + Google sign-in

Heavier setup but real-time multi-device push. Use this if you want changes to propagate instantly without manual pulls.

## What syncs

Everything personal: marks, bookmarks, notes, quests, settings, custom repos, custom palette. **Never** your API keys.

## Conflict resolution

Per-key last-write-wins. Two devices editing the same note simultaneously will keep the later write — open both, copy, merge by hand if needed.

## Privacy

For Gist mode, your token is stored locally (and synced to your own gist). For Firebase mode, data lives in your own Firebase project — CODEX is just a client.

articles.title: The Arrow-Pad & Every Shortcut
articles.body:
# The Arrow-Pad & Every Shortcut

Press **?** at any time for the live shortcut overlay. Everything below works whenever no overlay owns the keys.

## The arrow-pad — reading without reaching

- **↓ / ↑** — next / previous verse (the cursor flash follows)
- **→ / ←** — next / previous chapter
- **PgDn / PgUp** — scroll a screenful
- **Home / End** — first / last verse of the chapter
- **Enter** on a verse — open its verse menu

Vim hands work too: **J/K** verse, **H/L** chapter.

## Doors

- **⌘K** — the omnibar: references, panels, commands, questions
- **/** — smart search
- **1–9** — open that panel as its own window (desk) or sheet (phone)

## Instruments

- **O** — the Oracle
- **B** — marks (highlights, bookmarks, notes)
- **N** — toggle margin notes
- **M** — the Atlas for the current verse
- **T** — translations
- **S** — side-by-side translations
- **F** — focus: hide everything but the reader
- **Esc** — close the topmost overlay

## Settings

Settings itself is keyboard-first: ⌘K → `settings`, then type to filter; every control is reachable by Tab.

articles.title: Custom Bible Repositories
articles.body:
# Custom Bible Repositories

You can teach CODEX about a Bible translation it doesn't ship with — handy for less common editions or your own private translation hosted on a compatible API.

## Adding

Open **Settings → Bible → Custom repos → Add**. Provide:

- **Display name** — e.g. *My Translation*.
- **Provider** — one of the supported API shapes (bible-api.com, ESV, Sefaria, generic-JSON).
- **API id** — the identifier the provider expects (e.g. `web`, `kjv`, `wlc`).

The repo persists in `localStorage` and is merged into `CODEX_DATA.translations` on every boot. Chapter texts then ride the normal bible.js cache.

## Removing

The **Remove** action drops the repo from the translations list, deletes the persisted record, and **purges any cached chapters** so the slot is fully recoverable.

## Sharing

Custom repos are part of cross-device sync — set up once, get it everywhere.

articles.title: CODEX Terminal CLI
articles.body:
# CODEX CLI

A pure Node 18+ command-line client for reading scripture and running Oracle/panels from the shell. **Zero dependencies** — one file, no `node_modules` required.

## Why use the terminal?

The GUI is beautiful but the terminal is composable. Reach for the CLI when you want to:

- **Script a daily-reading email** — cron a verse out to yourself every morning, no browser needed.
- **Pipe scripture into other tools** — `node cli.js "Ps 1" | pandoc -o ps1.pdf` for instant typeset output.
- **Pair-program with scripture** — keep a verse in a tmux pane while you study its Greek next door.
- **Run on a headless server** — no display required; the entire CODEX brain reachable over SSH.
- **Build datasets** — `--json` for downstream analysis, fine-tuning, search indexing.
- **Integrate with your editor** — bind a key in vim, VS Code, or Cursor to fetch a verse into the buffer.

## First-time setup

1. Confirm Node 18+: `node --version`.
2. From the CODEX directory, mark the CLI executable: `chmod +x cli.js`.
3. (Optional) Add CODEX to your PATH so the CLI works from anywhere:

```
export PATH="$PATH:$HOME/path/to/codex"
# or symlink it for a short name:
ln -s "$HOME/path/to/codex/cli.js" /usr/local/bin/codex
```

4. (Optional, for AI features) Start the CODEX server in another shell: `node server.js`. The CLI defaults to `http://localhost:3001`. Without the server, the CLI still reads verses via `bible-api.com`.

## Reading verses

```
node cli.js "John 1:1-5"
node cli.js "Genesis 1" --translation kjv
node cli.js "Romans 8:28" --plain
```

## Oracle from the shell

```
node cli.js ask "What does logos mean in John 1:1?"
node cli.js ask "Compare KJV and NIV on Romans 5:12"
```

## Panels

```
node cli.js panel commentary "John 1:1"
node cli.js panel gematria  "Genesis 1:1"
```

## Flags

- `--server <url>` — override the CODEX server URL (e.g. point at a remote instance).
- `--translation <id>` — pick a translation (kjv, web, asv…).
- `--plain` — strip formatting, good for pipes and logs.
- `--no-color` — disable color but keep structure.
- `--json` — emit raw JSON for piping into `jq`, scripts, datasets.

## Real-world one-liners

Append today's reading to a journal file:

```
node cli.js "Ps 90:10" --plain | tee -a ~/journal.md
```

Get a verse, uppercase it, copy to clipboard (macOS):

```
node cli.js "Isa 40:31" --plain | tr '[:lower:]' '[:upper:]' | pbcopy
```

Stream a chapter into `less` for distraction-free reading in any terminal:

```
node cli.js "Romans 8" --plain | less -R
```

Extract just the Strong's numbers from a gematria panel:

```
node cli.js panel gematria "John 1:1" --json | jq '.lemmas[].strong'
```

## Daily-verse cron

A tiny crontab line that appends a verse to a daily log every morning at 7 am:

```
0 7 * * * cd /opt/codex && node cli.js daily --plain >> ~/daily.txt
```

Pair it with `mailx` to actually mail yourself the verse:

```
0 7 * * * cd /opt/codex && node cli.js daily --plain | mailx -s "Today's verse" you@example.com
```

## VS Code / Cursor integration

Open **Keyboard Shortcuts → keybindings.json** and bind a chord to fetch the current selection's reference (e.g. highlight `John 3:16`, hit the chord) into the integrated terminal:

```
{
  "key": "ctrl+alt+v",
  "command": "workbench.action.terminal.sendSequence",
  "args": { "text": "node cli.js \"${selectedText}\" --plain\u000D" }
}
```

## Vim integration

Map a leader key in your `.vimrc` to read the verse under the cursor and paste it below the current line:

```
nnoremap <leader>v :r !node ~/codex/cli.js "<C-r><C-w>" --plain<CR>
```

## tmux status-bar verse

Show a daily verse in your tmux status bar, refreshed once an hour:

```
set -g status-right '#(node /opt/codex/cli.js daily --plain | head -n1)'
set -g status-interval 3600
```

The terminal isn't a downgrade. It's CODEX without the chrome — every feature, none of the pixels.

articles.title: Plugins
articles.body:
# Plugins

Third parties (and our own future modules) can extend CODEX without touching core files. A plugin is a plain JS object pushed into `window.CODEX_PLUGINS` or registered via `window.CODEX_PLUGINS_API.register(plugin)`.

## Shape

```
{
  id: "strongs-concordance",
  name: "Strong's Concordance",
  version: "1.0.0",
  panels: [
    { id: "strongs", label: "Strong's", glyph: "ℋ", render(ctx) { ... } }
  ],
  verseActions: [
    { label: "Strong's Lookup", icon: "ℋ", handler(verseRef) { ... } }
  ],
  onNavigate(book, chapter) {},
  onVerseSelect(ref) {},
}
```

## Capabilities

- **Panels** — register new panels; each opens as its own window (desk) or sheet (phone).
- **Verse actions** — add rows to the verse menu.
- **Lifecycle hooks** — react to navigation and verse selection.
- **Quests** — register reading quests via `window.CODEX_QUESTS`.

## Installing

A plugin is just a script tag. Add it via **Settings → Plugins → Add script URL** and CODEX caches it offline. Future versions will support signed manifests and a permission prompt before first run.

articles.title: Data Modules
articles.body:
# Data Modules

Where **plugins** add code, **modules** add data — lexicons, cross-reference sets, commentaries, concordances. A module is a JSON file fetched once and stored in IndexedDB for offline use.

## Format

```
{
  "id": "strongs-greek",
  "kind": "lexicon",
  "name": "Strong's Greek Lexicon",
  "version": "1.0.0",
  "language": "el",
  "entries": { ... }
}
```

Supported `kind` values: `lexicon`, `cross-refs`, `commentary`, `concordance`, `red-letter`.

## Installing

**Settings → Modules → Add** and paste a URL to a JSON file. CODEX fetches, validates the shape, and stores it under `idb://codex.modules/<id>`. From then on it's available offline and surfaces in any panel that consumes that kind.

## Authoring

See `modules.js` in the source for the loader API and the validator. The same file exports for Node/CLI, so you can lint a module locally with `node modules.js validate my-lexicon.json`.

## Registry

A public registry is planned; for now, install from any URL you trust.

articles.title: Privacy & Data
articles.body:
# Privacy & Data

## What lives locally

Everything you do in CODEX is stored in **your browser** by default:

- Marks, bookmarks, notes, quests → `localStorage`
- Cached scripture, AI panels → IndexedDB / Cache Storage
- Settings, API keys → `localStorage` (`codex.tweaks.v1`, `codex.api.keys.v1`)
- Learned taste profile + Oracle chat context → on-device only, never uploaded

CODEX has no user accounts and no analytics.

## What gets sent for AI

Only when you actively use AI:

- **Oracle messages** — your prompt + recent chat + the current passage context
- **AI panel generation** — the verse text + a system prompt for that panel type
- **Compaction** — the chat transcript being condensed

No marks, no notes, no API keys, no identifying information are ever sent. Requests go through `/api/chat`.

## Clear profile & Oracle context

Reels builds a **private, on-device taste profile** from your likes and reading, and Oracle keeps a local chat context. Both stay on your device. To erase them, open **Settings → Clear profile & Oracle context**. This wipes personalization and chat memory **only** — your marks, notes, and cached scripture are untouched.

## Cross-device sync

Optional. If enabled, your settings + marks ride an end-to-end encrypted blob via your chosen sync backend (GitHub gist by default). You hold the key.

articles.title: Troubleshooting
articles.body:
# Troubleshooting

## A panel won't load / shows stale content

First try: tap the refresh icon on the panel itself — it bypasses the cache for that one verse.

## The app feels broken after an update

Hard refresh: `Cmd+Shift+R` (macOS) or `Ctrl+Shift+R` (Windows/Linux). This bypasses the service worker for a single load.

## Reset the service worker

DevTools → Application → Service Workers → **Unregister**. Then reload. CODEX will reinstall fresh on next visit.

## Clear all caches

Settings → Cache → **Clear cached scripture / panels**. Surgical: leaves your marks intact.

## Factory reset

Settings → Danger Zone → **Reset factory settings**. Wipes preferences and API keys; keeps marks, notes, cached scripture.

## Still broken?

Open the browser console. CODEX logs prefixed `[codex]` are usually informative.

articles.title: For Jewish Readers
articles.body:
# For Jewish Readers

CODEX is built ecumenically. For a Jewish reading experience:

## Recommended setup

- Install **WLC** (Westminster Leningrad Codex, pointed Hebrew) as the primary translation.
- Enable **YHWH restoration** in Settings → Reading.
- Keep the **Talmud** panel's window open on your desk.
- Hide the New Testament books in Settings → Reading → Canon filter.

## Coming soon

- **Parsha cycle** — auto-jump to this week's Torah portion.
- **Hebrew calendar** — Shabbat-aware reading reminders.
- **Talmud daf** — direct Sefaria-style links from any verse cited in Mishnah/Gemara.
- **Targum overlay** — Aramaic Targum Onkelos / Jonathan as a secondary translation.

Feedback welcome — open an issue on the project repo.

articles.title: For Academic Study
articles.body:
# For Academic Study

## Original-language workflow

- Install **WLC** (Hebrew) and **Tisch** or **NA28** (Greek) translations.
- Enable **Side-by-side** (`S`) with the original alongside your working English.
- Use the **Gematria** panel for Hebrew numerical analysis; tap a word for lexicon entries.

## Manuscript variants

The **Apologetics** panel surfaces major textual variants for the active verse — which manuscripts read what, and how modern critical editions adjudicate. For deeper apparatus work, the LXX install includes Rahlfs sigla.

## Translation analysis

Ask Oracle: *“Compare how KJV, NIV, NRSV, and the WLC handle the syntax of this verse.”* Oracle has access to every translation you've installed.

## Citations

The verse menu's **Cite** action produces SBL-format citations including translation, edition, and access date.

articles.title: Community Modules
articles.body:
# Community Modules (Preview)

CODEX has a **module system** that lets third parties ship study tools — panels, overlays, quests, lexicons — without forking the app.

## How it will work

A module is a single signed `.module.json` file describing:

- The kind (panel | overlay | quest | translation source)
- The endpoint or static data it provides
- Permissions it requests (read passage? send to remote?)

Install via Settings → Modules → **Add module** and paste a URL. CODEX verifies the signature, shows the permission prompt, and caches the module offline.

## Current status

The module loader (`modules.js`) is present but the public registry is not yet open. Watch the project README for updates.

## Building one

See `modules.js` and the existing built-in panels in `panels-gen.js` for the API surface.

articles.title: The Thread Web — Cross-References
articles.body:
# The Thread Web

The **Treasury of Scripture Knowledge** is a 19th-century public-domain index that maps every verse in the Bible to its thematic and parallel passages — hundreds of thousands of threads. CODEX renders it as the **Thread Web**: a living ego-graph, not a concordance list.

## How it works

1. Click any verse, then choose **Cross-References** from the verse menu (or ⌘K → `crossrefs`).
2. The current verse sits at the **center**; its cross-references **orbit** it on a canvas, ordered canonically. Color speaks covenant: Old Testament nodes are cyan, New Testament amber — and an edge that crosses the testament seam **burns gold**.
3. **Click an orbit node** and it becomes the new center; your walked trail renders as a clickable breadcrumb under the canvas.
4. **Double-click** a node to jump the reader there.
5. Tap the center node (or the ¶ chip) and the verse's full prose unfolds beneath the canvas — the text is the zoom-in.

## Honesty

The threads are the real TSK adjacency, computed in front of you — no precomputed mystery blob. TSK carries no strength votes, so orbit distance is canonical order, not invented "relevance".

A visually-hidden list of real buttons mirrors the graph for keyboards and screen readers.

articles.title: The Galaxy — the Canon as One Sky
articles.body:
# The Galaxy

⌘K → `galaxy` (or ❂ in the Palm). Every cross-reference in the Treasury of Scripture Knowledge — hundreds of thousands of threads — as navigable 3D space. The 1,189 chapters of the canon are **stars**, clustered by their natural families, linked by every thread.

## Flying

- **Drag** to orbit · **scroll** to dive · **click a star** to approach it.
- **Double-click a star** → the reader flies to that chapter.
- **PATH** "Gen 1 → Rev 21" → the route burns gold through space. Paths prefer heavily-attested links — scholarship, not trivia.
- **NEAR** "Isaiah 53" → that chapter's ego-network ignites and the camera flies in.
- **FAMILIES** → stars recolor by their label-propagation clusters.

## Reading the sky

Old Testament stars speak **cyan**, New Testament **amber** — and a thread between testaments goes **gold**: the seam of the covenants, visible. Your own reading trail burns gold through the stars (local, never uploaded).

## The star dossier

**Click a star** and a real dossier opens — everything the graph knows about that chapter: its strongest threads, its family, its place in the sky, and **the Word itself**, loaded chapter text included.

## Honesty

The threads are the real public-domain TSK adjacency, aggregated in front of you with a progress readout — no precomputed mystery blob. The layout caches locally so reopening is instant.

articles.title: About CODEX
articles.body:
# About CODEX

## Vision

CODEX is a **distraction-respectful** Bible study environment that treats scripture as the center of gravity and AI as a humble assistant — not the other way around. No notifications. No engagement metrics. No upsells.

## What makes it different

- **Offline-first**: a fully working Bible app even on an airplane.
- **Translation-rich**: dozens of editions including Hebrew, Greek, Latin, Ethiopian.
- **AI panels**: per-verse Talmud, Commentary, Gematria, Gnosis, Apologetics — cached, not re-billed.
- **Cross-tradition**: useful to Protestants, Catholics, Orthodox, Jewish readers, academics, and the curious skeptic alike.

## Open source

CODEX is open source. The repository hosts the React PWA, the proxy server, and the panel/quest definitions. Contributions — translations, panels, quests, fixes — are welcome.

## Credits

Built with React, Babel, and Leaflet. Bible texts are public domain or used per their respective licenses (acknowledged in `data/bibles/`).

articles.title: Reels — Scriptural Scroll
articles.body:
# Reels — Endless Scriptural Scroll

A **TikTok-shaped vertical feed** for scripture: hand-crafted cards (symbols, names of God, did-you-knows, prophecy pairs, three-sentence parables, calligraphic verses, numerology, questions, quest teases) interleaved with AI-generated cards built from your active reading. **Always something new** to discover in a 30-second window.

## How to open

Open **REELS** from ⌘K or the dock (glyph ⬚). Tap the ⛶ icon to go fullscreen.

## Navigate

- **Mobile**: swipe up / down
- **Desktop**: scroll wheel, `↑` / `↓`, `j` / `k`, `Space`, `PageUp` / `PageDown`
- `Esc` exits fullscreen

## Each card has

- A type badge (top-left)
- A bottom action bar:
  - **♡** save the anchor verse to bookmarks
  - **📖** open the anchor passage in the reader
  - **🪶 plain version** — when applicable, translates dense text into normie-friendly everyday language (uses your active UI language)
  - **⤴** copy the card text

## Card types

Symbol, Name of God, Did You Know, Parable in 3 Sentences, Prophecy → Fulfillment, Counting (numerology), Question (tap to reveal), Quest tease, Light Verse (pure typography), Art Verse (Wikimedia painting + verse overlay).

## Personalization

Reels learns what you respond to. Your **likes**, your **highlights**, and your **reading history** build a private, on-device taste profile that gently biases the feed toward the books and card types you actually engage with. Nothing leaves the device — the profile is computed and stored locally, never uploaded. To start fresh, open **Settings → Clear profile & Oracle context**, which erases the learned profile (and the saved chat context) while leaving your marks, notes, and cached scripture intact.

## Pre-loading

While you read a chapter, Reels silently builds a queue of ~30 cards in the background, drawing on your already-cached verse art and panel data. When you open Reels, the queue is ready instantly.

## No repetition

Served cards are remembered for ~2,000 entries so you don't see the same thing twice in a session. The queue resets when fully consumed.

articles.title: Full-Text Search
articles.body:
# Full-Text Search

Press `⌘K` (Mac) or `Ctrl+K` (Windows/Linux) anywhere in CODEX to open the **scripture search bar**. Type a word — results appear instantly across every translation you have cached.

## What it searches

Every verse from every translation already downloaded to this device. The index is built locally from your offline Bible cache — no network required. New chapters you open are folded into the index automatically.

## Query syntax

- **Plain words** — `love your enemies` finds verses containing all three.
- **Phrases** — wrap in double quotes for exact contiguity: `"in the beginning"`.
- **Wildcard suffix** — trailing `*` matches any continuation: `lov*` matches *love*, *loved*, *lovingkindness*.
- **Translation filter** — prefix with `@TRANS` to restrict: `@KJV love` or `@WLC bereshit`.

## Keyboard

- `↑` / `↓` move the selection between results.
- `Enter` opens the highlighted verse (loads the chapter and lands on the verse).
- `Esc` closes the overlay.

## Privacy & offline

The index lives in your browser (IndexedDB). Nothing is sent to any server. The first search after a fresh install scans the cache once (~100ms) and persists the doc list, so subsequent reloads are instant.

articles.title: Strong's Concordance
articles.body:
# Strong's Concordance

Strong's numbers are a 19th-century numbering system that assigns every distinct word in the Hebrew Old Testament (H1–H8674) and Greek New Testament (G1–G5624) a unique ID. Two English words that look identical can map to *different* original-language lemmas — and Strong's lets you see that instantly.

## Using the panel

Open the **Strong's** panel (⌘K → `strongs`, or its dock chip). For the verse you're reading you'll see every aligned word listed with its translit, gloss, and Strong's number. Tap a word to expand the full entry: the original-language form, pronunciation, part of speech, definition, and a usage count across scripture.

## Direct lookup

The search box at the top accepts any Strong's number — `H430` (Elohim), `G2316` (theos), or just `2316` (we guess H vs. G by range). Useful when a commentary or footnote cites a number.

## Partial lexicon

This first release ships a **starter set of ~500 high-frequency entries** (God, love, faith, spirit, light, holy, righteousness, etc.). The full ~14,000-entry lexicon is a follow-up; entries not yet bundled show a friendly "not in starter set" notice.

articles.title: The Help Wiki Itself
articles.body:
# The Help Wiki Itself

A small recursion: documentation for the documentation. This article explains how the Help Wiki works so you can get the most out of it.

## Four ways to find an answer

1. **Predictive search** — start typing in the search box. The top five matching articles appear instantly, ranked by title hits first, then tags, then body. Each result shows a snippet with your query underlined so you can scan without clicking.
2. **Press ↵** — hits the top match. Saves you a mouse trip.
3. **Ask Oracle** — for free-form questions where no single article is the answer. Oracle gets the **entire help corpus** as context and answers using only what's in it; if the answer isn't here, Oracle says so plainly and points to the nearest article.
4. **Browse by category** — eight categories, each with an icon, a one-line blurb, and an article count. Click a card to drill in.

## Reading an article

- The **scroll-progress bar** at the top tracks how far you've read.
- **prev / next** in the top bar walks you through the current category in order.
- A **related articles** strip at the bottom suggests siblings that share tags with the one you're reading.
- Code blocks have a **copy button** in the top-right corner.
- Blockquotes render as pull quotes.

## Translation

The **🌐 Translate** menu in the article bar runs the current article through the AI into any of eight languages (Spanish, German, Portuguese, French, Latin, Hebrew, Greek, Hindi). Translations are cached in `localStorage` forever, so revisiting a translated article is instant and offline-safe. A small ● next to a language means "already cached." Predictive search also indexes your cached translations — non-English users can search the wiki in their own tongue.

## Where the articles live

Every article in this wiki is a markdown body inside `data/help/articles.json`. Non-engineers can ship doc updates in a release without touching React.

## When something is missing

If you can't find what you need: ask Oracle. If Oracle can't find it either, the answer is probably not yet documented — file it in the project repo and we'll add it.

articles.title: Interlinear Mode
articles.body:
# Interlinear Mode

When interlinear mode is on, each aligned English word in the KJV is rendered with its **Strong's number tucked beneath** and the original-language lemma in the hover tooltip. Tap any word to jump straight to its entry in the Strong's panel.

## Translation support

Alignment is **KJV-only** for now — that's the translation the public-domain alignments cover most reliably. Switching to another translation falls back to plain rendering.

## Sample coverage

This release ships a proof-of-concept alignment for a handful of well-known chapters: **Genesis 1, Psalm 23, John 1, John 3, and Romans 8**. Verses outside that set render normally with no interlinear underlay until the full alignment lands in a future update.

## Tips

- Tap a word once to open its entry. Tap a second time to dismiss.
- Long Strong's entries are scrollable — drag to read the full definition.

articles.title: Tourist Mode
articles.body:
# Tourist Mode

Open any verse map (tap a verse → MAP) and hit the **○ TOURIST** button in the map's control bar. CODEX will ask for your current location and surface biblical / sacred-text / historical sites within 50 km — ranked by significance.

## What you get

- A **PLACES NEAR YOU** side panel with card-by-card descriptions, distance, era, what to see, best time of day to visit, and walking-route hints.
- **Pins on the map** for each place. Click a card to recenter and drop a dashed breadcrumb line from your location to the destination.
- *If you only have an hour* — an AI-ranked top 3 by accessibility plus meaning.
- *Deeper rabbit hole* — one less-visited, historically rich site worth the detour.

## Privacy + caching

Your GPS coordinates are sent to your AI provider once per fetch. Results are cached locally for 24 hours, keyed to your location rounded to ~110 m — return visits inside that radius reuse the cached set, no extra calls.

## If location is denied

The panel shows a clear message with a Retry button. You can grant location in your browser settings and try again. Tourist mode never falls back to IP geolocation.

articles.title: Map Magic — Discoveries, Overlays & Chrono
articles.body:
# Map Magic

The verse map is more than a pin — it's an explorable, era-aware atlas of the biblical world.

## Discoverable sites (Pokemon-GO-ish)

In **Tourist mode** the map shows every well-known biblical site as a faint dot. When you're physically within 1 km of one, it **pulses gold** with a *DISCOVERABLE* label. Tap to discover — the AI writes a short second-person narrative (*"You are standing where Paul addressed the Areopagus…"*) and the site joins your **pilgrimage log**, persisted to local storage as `codex.discovered`. The corner counter (🏛 N) tracks how many you've found.

## Layer toggles

Five overlays in the control bar:

- **✦ Biblical events** — the AI-fetched POIs for the current verse (default on).
- **◯ Pilgrimage routes** — Via Dolorosa, Camino de Santiago, Jesus Trail, the Hajj approach.
- **⬡ Manuscript discoveries** — Qumran (Dead Sea Scrolls), Nag Hammadi, St. Catherine's, Cairo Geniza, Oxyrhynchus.
- **☰ Empire borders** — AI-generated polygon for the major empire controlling the current map view at the year on the slider.
- **⚐ My discoveries** — your personal pilgrimage log.

## AI-intelligent timeline

The CHRONO slider does more than scrub. Drag it and a **WHEN** badge appears with a one-line headline of the political/religious situation at that year and three contemporary nearby events. Drag to 587 BCE over Jerusalem and you'll see Babylonian siege under Nebuchadnezzar · Temple destroyed · Lamentations composed. Fetches are debounced (~320 ms) and cached per decade.

## Voice tour

Every tourist popup and discovery card has a **▶ PLAY TOUR** button. CODEX narrates the description aloud using your browser's native speech synthesis — no API cost, works offline, uses your UI language. Tap again to interrupt.

## Readable timeline labels

Major labels (2K BC, 1K BC, 0, 1K, 2K) are always shown. A ResizeObserver dims alternates when the slider gets narrow so labels never overlap. Minor 250-year ticks render as dashes; polity boundaries get a tiny accent line — no labels — to keep the bar uncluttered.

articles.title: Gematria Intelligence & Kabbalah
articles.body:
# Gematria Intelligence

The Gematria panel is more than a calculator. It computes every classical numerological system on demand, cross-references your library, and now opens a Kabbalistic compartment for verses that warrant one.

## Systems supported

Hebrew: **Mispar Hechrachi** (standard), **Gadol** (finals lifted), **Sidduri** (ordinal), **Katan** (digit-reduced), **Katan Mispari** (digital root), **Bone'eh** (cumulative), **Kidmi** (triangular), **Atbash**, **Albam**, **Ne'elam**, **Ha'akhor**.

Greek: **Isopsephy** (classical α=1 … ω=800), **Ordinal**, **Reduced**.

English: **Ordinal**, **Reduction**, **Reverse**.

All systems compute **on-device, offline**.

## Clickable everywhere

Every numerical value, every original-language word, and every cross-reference is interactive.

- **Click a value** — opens a popover listing every verse in your cached library that sums to the same number, plus its symbolic meaning when one exists (e.g. 358 → Mashiach / Nachash).
- **Click a word** — opens the Strong's entry for that lemma.
- **Click a reference** — jumps to that passage in the reader.

Keyboard users: every clickable element is focusable; **Enter** or **Space** activates it.

## Library cross-matching

The **FROM YOUR LIBRARY** tab scans every chapter you've cached (Hebrew, Greek, English) and groups them by gematria value. The more you read, the richer the index becomes.

## Kabbalistic layer

At the bottom, a **⟁ KABBALAH · HIDDEN COMPARTMENT** appears whenever the verse echoes a Kabbalistic structure:

- **Sefirot resonance** — when a computed value matches one of the ten spheres (e.g. 72 → Chesed, 67 → Binah, 496 → Malkhut, 620 → Keter). A mini Tree of Life highlights the active sphere.
- **Concept echoes** — values that map to well-known Kabbalistic concepts: 13 (Echad / Ahavah), 26 (YHWH), 248 (positive mitzvot), 365 (negative mitzvot), 613 (total Torah), 666 (the beast), 888 (Iēsous in Greek).
- **Lurianic frame** — when relevant, the verse is read through *tzimtzum* (contraction), *shevirat ha-kelim* (shattering), *tikkun* (mending), or *gilgul* (soul-rotation).
- **Partzufim** — the divine 'faces' (Atik Yomin, Abba, Ima, Zeir Anpin, Nukva) when the verse points to one.
- **Zohar citations** — actual references from the Zohar and Tikkunei Zohar when the AI knows them.

This layer is interpretive, not doctrinal — collapse the section to dismiss.

articles.title: Exegesis · Deep Analysis
articles.body:
# Exegesis Panel

The **✎ EXEGESIS** panel goes a layer deeper than Commentary. Where Commentary surfaces four voices from across church history, Exegesis runs a single structured scholarly pass over the passage and returns a working analyst's brief.

## What it gives you

Each run produces seven collapsible sections:

- **Key terms** — the original Hebrew/Greek words that matter, with transliteration, semantic range, and how the major translations have handled them.
- **Literary structure** — chiasm, parallelism, narrative arc — the shape of the passage.
- **Historical context** — who, when, where, why now.
- **Intertextual echoes** — what this text quotes, fulfills, or inverts elsewhere in scripture (refs are jump-clickable).
- **Exegetical options** — competing scholarly readings with the names behind each.
- **Preferred reading** — a single most-defensible synthesis.
- **Theological implication** and **Applicational pivot** — what it means and how it lands.

## When to use it

Use it **before sermon prep, exegesis papers, or any deep study session**. Use Commentary first for the panoramic survey, then open Exegesis for the technical layer. Both cache independently in localStorage — once drafted for a chapter, returning is offline-instant.

Hit **REDRAFT** to force a fresh pass with your current provider/model (set in Settings → Oracle).

articles.title: Translation Analysis
articles.body:
# Translation Analysis Panel

The **⟷ TRANSLATIONS·** tab compares every translation you currently have loaded for one verse — and explains exactly *why* they disagree.

## How it activates

Open the **Translations** tab first and check at least one comparison translation alongside your primary. The Translation Analysis tab only activates when **2 or more translations** are loaded. Otherwise it shows a friendly placeholder asking you to add more.

## What it produces

Three collapsible sections:

- **Comparison table** — every loaded rendering with its year, philosophy (formal / dynamic / paraphrase / interlinear), the rendered text, and the key lexical or syntactic choice that distinguishes it.
- **Divergence points** — the underlying Hebrew/Greek ambiguities or interpretive cruxes that force translators to pick a path, with each path traced to the philosophy that drives it.
- **Recommendations** — which loaded translation is best for **study**, for **devotion**, and for staying **closest to the source text**.

## When to use it

Use it **anytime you wonder why two Bibles read differently** — favourite verses that hit different in NIV vs ESV, Romans 5:12 "because" vs "in that whom", John 1:1 word order in interlinear vs paraphrase. It's also a great teaching tool: paste the table into a small-group lesson plan.

Each unique combination of translations is cached separately — switching which translations are loaded re-checks the cache before fetching.

articles.title: Schizo Mode
articles.body:
# Schizo Mode ⚯

*Here is wisdom. Let him that hath understanding count the number of the beast…* — **Revelation 13:18**

Schizo Mode is a hidden numerology overlay. The settings toggle only appears once you've actually visited Revelation 13:18 — a nod to the verse's invitation to *count*. Once you've unlocked it, the toggle stays available forever; flipping it on dresses the reader in a thematic skin around a set of real, useful study tools.

## How to unlock

1. Navigate to **Revelation 13** and scroll verse 18 into view.
2. Open **Settings → Look**. A new toggle — **Schizo Mode** — appears at the bottom of the section with a faint glitch flicker on its label.
3. Flip it on.

The unlock is sticky across sessions; the toggle is stored locally as `codex.schizo.eligible`.

## What it does

- **Gematria margins** — every verse gets a tiny floating numerical value to its left, computed from the primary classical system (Hechrachi for Hebrew, isopsephy for Greek, ordinal for English). Hover any value to see every system at once.
- **Significant-number glow** — values that hit known symbolic totals pulse with a coloured halo: **666** red, **888** gold (Iēsous in isopsephy), **358** cyan (Mashiach / Nachash), **144** violet (sealed), **153** blue (the fish), **777** white, plus accent glow for 7, 12, 40, 70, 1000, 1776.
- **Cipher search** — press `=` anywhere outside an input. You'll be prompted for a number; CODEX scans the gematria index across every cached verse in your library and offers to jump to the first match. A `codex:cipher-search` event also fires for any listener you write.
- **Visual tone** — faint matrix-green wash on dark mode, subtle scanlines even when the regular scanlines tweak is off, slightly looser letter-spacing on verse numbers, and a small **⚯** sigil in the bottom-right corner indicating the mode is live.

## Philosophy

These are real numerological study tools dressed in a thematic skin. The trigger verse is just a nod to Rev 13:18's invitation: *let him that hath understanding count*. Use it for serious gematria work, ironic ambience, or both.

articles.title: Bible Dictionary
articles.body:
# Bible Dictionary (ℵ)

The **DICT** panel opens **Easton's Bible Dictionary** (M.G. Easton, 1893 — public domain), the classic one-volume reference for every name, place, concept, and people-group in scripture. It is bundled offline, with no network required.

## Three ways in

- **Search** — start typing in the search box. Top five matches surface live across titles, related entries, and body text.
- **Browse by letter** — tap any letter A–Z to list every entry beginning with it. Dim letters have no entries in this sample.
- **From a verse** — open the verse menu on any verse and choose *Look up in Dictionary* to auto-search the first proper noun.

## Auto-surfaced for your chapter

When you open a chapter, the panel scans its verses for proper nouns and shows a **Dictionary picks for this chapter** strip at the top — usually the 2–4 most-mentioned figures or places, one tap away.

## Entries

Each entry shows the original etymology, a 100–200-word article in dignified 19th-century prose, scripture chips that jump straight to the reader, and **related entries** you can click to chain through (a back button preserves your trail).

## Sample vs. full

This build ships **~200 well-curated entries** of Easton's ~4,000 — covering every major figure (Adam through Paul), the principal places (Eden, Sinai, Bethlehem, Rome), the great concepts (Covenant, Atonement, Resurrection), and the peoples (Hebrews, Philistines, Samaritans, Gentiles). When you search for something not yet seeded, a **Search Wikipedia** link appears as a one-tap fallback.

articles.title: Contributing to CODEX
articles.body:
# Contributing to CODEX

CODEX is open source and explicitly designed to be extended by anyone — code, data, or translations. There is **no build step** and **no npm dependencies** in the runtime: clone the repo, run `node server.js`, refresh.

## The four reference docs

Four Markdown files at the repo root tell you everything you need:

- **[CONTRIBUTING.md](CONTRIBUTING.md)** — project philosophy, file map, coding conventions, commit style, pull-request etiquette, code of conduct. Start here.
- **[SPEC.md](SPEC.md)** — the formal extension specification: the three extension surfaces (plugins, modules, translations), the plugin lifecycle, the module loader, the event bus, every window global, the localStorage / IndexedDB / service-worker schemas, the `/api/*` server endpoints, and the versioning policy.
- **[MODULES.md](MODULES.md)** — practical, author-friendly guide for writing data modules: the meta envelope, the ten module types (lexicon, concordance, cross-reference, commentary, reading-plan, timeline, map-overlay, dictionary, parsha, cantillation) with worked examples and local-testing instructions.
- **[API.md](API.md)** — flat alphabetical reference for every `window.CODEX_*` global and every `codex:*` custom event, with one-line signatures. Useful as a quick lookup while coding.

## Three ways to contribute

1. **Modules (data, JSON only)** — easiest. Add lexicons, cross-references, commentaries, plans, maps. See `data/modules/` for live examples.
2. **Plugins (code)** — extend the panel surface and verse menu without touching core files. A six-line plugin is in `plugins.js`.
3. **Translations** — add new Bible texts via the registry in `bible.js`.

## House rules in one paragraph

Vanilla React via Babel-standalone. JSX in `.jsx`, plain JS in `.js`. No TypeScript. No bundler. Keep state under the `codex.*` localStorage namespace; for plugins use `codex.plugin.<your-id>.*`. Version every cache key. Comment the *why*, not the *what*. Be kind in PR review.

articles.title: Semantic Search · Concept Mode
articles.body:
# Semantic Search

Open search with `Cmd/Ctrl+K`. At the top of the modal you'll see two tabs: **TEXT** and **CONCEPT**.

## TEXT vs CONCEPT

- **TEXT** is the classic full-text search — keyword, `"exact phrase"`, wildcard (`lov*`), and `@KJV` translation filter. Instant, offline, exhaustive.
- **CONCEPT** is AI-powered. Ask in plain language and get up to 15 conceptually relevant passages ranked by relevance, each with a short *why* note and a 5-dot relevance bar.

## Example queries

- *Jesus on prayer*
- *wisdom literature on suffering*
- *passages about light vs darkness*
- *shepherd metaphors*
- *forgiveness across both testaments*

Click any result to jump straight to that verse.

## Caching & offline

Every concept query is cached in localStorage keyed by query + UI language. Re-asking is **instant and works offline** — useful for revisiting your favourite topical clusters on a plane.

## Requirements

Concept mode needs an AI key for any configured provider. Set one up in **Settings → AI Engines**. If no key is configured you'll see a friendly nudge and a suggestion to switch back to TEXT. Your last-used mode is remembered between sessions.

articles.title: Passage Guide
articles.body:
# Passage Guide ❖

The **Passage Guide** is CODEX's single-page synthesis for the current chapter — every CODEX module pulled into one beautiful, printed-study-sheet view. Think of it as the front matter you'd want for any chapter you sit with.

## Opening it

Open any chapter, then open the **GUIDE** panel (⌘K → `guide`). The guide loads instantly from cache if you've opened this chapter before; otherwise it generates in a few seconds.

## What's inside

1. **Hero** — chapter title, AI overview, verse count.
2. **Outline** — 4–6 numbered sections with verse ranges. Click one to jump.
3. **Key Themes** — chip cards for the chapter's dominant motifs. Click to jump to the anchor verse.
4. **Top Cross-References** — pulled live from the TSK cross-reference module, deduped and grouped by theme.
5. **Word Studies** — five most significant original-language words. Click a Strong's number to open the Strong's panel.
6. **Numerology Snapshot** — gematria values in this chapter that hit symbolic totals (358 Mashiach, 666 the beast, 144 sealed, etc.), pulled from the kabbalah-mappings module.
7. **Historical Context** — a short scholarly paragraph on setting.
8. **Geography** — one-click pass-through to the verse map.
9. **Synthesis** — one beautiful paragraph weaving outline, themes, and context into the chapter's arc.
10. **Related Reading** — three cards drawn from the Reels curated set, prioritising other chapters of the same book.

## Cost & caching

Exactly **one** AI call per chapter — the outline, themes, key words, history, and synthesis arrive in a single JSON object. Cross-refs, word lookups, and numerology are pulled synchronously from already-loaded modules and cost nothing. The full guide is cached under `codex.passage-guide.{book}.{chapter}` and survives reloads. Use **↻ Regenerate** in the hero to refresh.

## Tips

- Sections are collapsible — tap any header.
- Themes and outline ranges are clickable navigation, not decoration.
- Word-study Strong's chips only light up when the Strong's lexicon has loaded; cold-open the Strong's panel once to warm it.
- The guide respects your active language: switch UI language and re-open to regenerate in the new language.

articles.title: Reading Plans
articles.body:
# Reading Plans

The **PLANS** panel turns CODEX into a daily-cadence companion. Pick a plan, tap **Begin today**, and CODEX serves you the right passages every day — with a streak counter, calendar heat-map, and catch-up button when life gets in the way.

## Today view

For your active plan, today's readings are listed as clickable links. Tap a reading to jump straight to that chapter; tap the circle to check it off. When every reading is checked, the day turns cyan on the calendar and your streak grows by one.

## Streak & catch-up

The 🔥 streak counts consecutive completed days. If you miss a day, you'll see a banner: *“You're 3 days behind.”* Tap **Catch up** to bulk-mark missed days, or quietly read them when you can — your call.

## The seven bundled plans

CODEX ships with both Christian and Jewish cycles by default:

- **Canonical — 1 Year** · Genesis → Revelation in order
- **Chronological — 1 Year** · ordered by when events likely occurred
- **Whole Bible — 90 Day Sprint** · ~13 chapters/day
- **Gospels — 90 Days** · Matthew, Mark, Luke, John
- **Psalms & Proverbs — 30 Days** · 5 psalms + 1 proverbs/day
- **Daf Yomi — Bavli Talmud (preview)** · the 7.5-year folio cycle
- **Torah — Triennial Cycle (preview)** · each parshah across three years

Daf Yomi and Triennial ship as 90-day previews; full cycles arrive in a later release.

## Reminders
