Tap **Daily reminder…** to set a notification time. CODEX will ping you while the app is open. (System-level reminders for a closed PWA arrive in a future Capacitor build.)

articles.title: Sermon & Study Builder
articles.body:
# Sermon & Study Builder

The **STUDIES** panel (glyph `❡`) is a clipboard for sermon prep and structured study notes. Assemble verses, free-form notes, panel excerpts, and cross-references into named sections, then export the result.

## Studies & sections

- Tap **+ New** to create a study; tap any row in the list to switch active. The **×** deletes it.
- The active study lives at the bottom: edit its **title** inline, add **sections** with `+ Section`, and rename section headings directly.
- Drag the **⋮⋮** grip on a section or item to reorder. You can drag items between sections too.

## Item types

- **Verse** — ref badge + translation + scripture text. Add via the verse menu’s **Add to Study** action, or `+ Verse from current` inside any section.
- **Note** — free-form, editable inline. Supports `**bold**` and `*italic*` on export.
- **Panel excerpt** — any commentary, Gematria, or Apologetics panel with an **Add to Study** button drops a snippet here, tagged with its source.
- **Cross-reference** — added from the CROSS-REFS panel; carries an optional note.

## Export & share

The footer offers **Markdown** (download `.md`), **PDF** (uses your browser's print dialog with a clean print stylesheet), **Copy** (Markdown to clipboard), **Share** (Web Share API or a `?study=` URL fallback), and **.codex-study** (JSON for backup).

## Import

Drop a `.codex-study` file onto the STUDIES panel, or open a `?study=…` URL — CODEX will decode it into a new study automatically.

articles.title: Browser Extension
articles.body:
# Browser Extension

The **CODEX Browser Extension** turns any web page into a launchpad for scripture lookup. Install it once and three things become possible:

## 1. Highlight → right-click → look up

Select any text on a page, right-click, choose **“Look up '…' in CODEX”**. If the selection contains a scripture reference (any format — `John 3:16`, `1 Cor 13:4-7`, `Ps 23`, `Gn 1:1`), CODEX opens directly to that passage in a new tab. If no reference is detected, the toolbar popup shows a friendly hint instead of opening blindly.

The reference parser understands the full 66-book Protestant canon plus common deuterocanonicals (Tobit, Judith, Wisdom, Sirach, Baruch, 1–2 Maccabees), and dozens of abbreviation variants — KJV-style, SBL-style, single-letter shorthand, roman numerals on numbered books.

## 2. Toolbar popup search

Click the CODEX icon in your browser toolbar to get a small search bar. Type any passage and hit Enter to open it in CODEX, or click **Peek verse** to read the text inline without leaving the page. The popup also remembers your last five lookups for one-tap recall.

If you recently highlighted a reference on the page, the popup auto-prefills with it — no retyping.

## 3. Verse of the day (optional new tab)

The extension ships with an **optional** new-tab page that displays a beautifully typeset verse of the day, rotating daily from a curated set of 30 well-known passages. Click through to open it in CODEX. A small lookup bar sits underneath for quick reference jumps.

The new-tab override is **opt-in**. If you'd rather keep your browser's default new tab, delete the `chrome_url_overrides` block from `manifest.json` (or use the bundled `manifest-newtab.json` variant) and reload the extension.

## Install

The extension lives in the `/extension/` directory of the CODEX repository. See [`extension/README.md`](../../extension/README.md) for step-by-step install instructions for Chrome, Edge, Firefox, and Safari (developer mode and store submission).

## Privacy

Zero data collection. Zero analytics. Zero trackers. The only network requests it makes are to **bible-api.com** (when you peek a verse or load the new-tab page) and to **codex.app** (when you click through). Recent lookups live only in your browser's local storage and never leave your machine.

articles.title: AI Study Quests
articles.body:
# AI Study Quests ⚔

**AI Study Quests** turn any theme you can name into a custom 5–8 step guided tour through scripture — with questions, hints, and a closing AI reflection.

## Starting a quest

Open the **QUESTS** panel (⌘K → `quests`, or its dock chip) and pick *AI Study Quests · custom guided tours*. You'll land on the catalog.

## The catalog

- **Suggested quests** — chip buttons for common themes like *Trace covenant from Abraham to Christ* or *Names of God in the Pentateuch*. Tap one to generate.
- **Free-form** — type any theme (*"the role of bread from Eden to Emmaus"*) and hit **Generate**. CODEX designs a quest in a few seconds and returns a preview card listing the steps. Click **Begin** when you're ready — quests don't auto-fire.

## The answer flow

Each step shows you a passage (click the ref to jump to the reader), a short intro, and an open question. Write your response in the box — CODEX auto-saves as you go. Stuck? Tap **Show hint** for guidance that points without giving the answer. **Next** advances; **Previous** goes back. Progress is persisted, so an interrupted quest resumes exactly where you left off.

## AI feedback at the end

After the last step you reach a **Reflection** page. Write a paragraph tying the thread together, then tap **Get AI feedback** — CODEX reads every answer and returns thoughtful, scripture-rich feedback plus one follow-up reading suggestion.

## Saving & sharing

**Save quest** drops the finished tour (your answers and the feedback included) into **My quests** at the bottom of the catalog. **Share** copies a URL hash that recreates the quest on someone else's device. Quests need an AI key configured in **Settings → AI Engines**.

articles.title: The River — the Biblical Timeline
articles.body:
# The River

⌘K → `timeline` opens the biblical timeline as a **living river** — the axis itself is the instrument.

## Moving on the river

- **Wheel or pinch to zoom**, centered on your cursor — no zoom buttons.
- **Drag to pan**, with inertia (inertia is disabled under reduced-motion).
- Era strata breathe behind the river; events are **sized by significance** — significance meaning breadth of attestation (refs + people + places), not editorial taste.
- Dense stretches fold into **+N cluster nodes** that unfold as you dive in.
- A **minimap strip** at the foot shows the whole river at a glance — drag it to fly.

## Reading the river

- **Click an event** — it centers, and an in-river detail card opens (never a modal) carrying live scripture chips.
- **Double-click** an event and the reader jumps to its primary reference.
- The gold **◈ NOW-READING marker** tracks what you're reading. It is marked ≈ because verse→year mapping is approximate by nature — and says so.

## Honesty

The **SCHOLARLY SURVEY · NOT PREDICTION** banner stays. Every date is "c." — conventional chronology, contested where scholars contest it.

articles.title: Jewish Study Tools
articles.body:
# Jewish Study Tools ה

The **TORAH** panel gathers the weekly Jewish lectionary, Hebrew-calendar context, holiday awareness, and Daf Yomi into one panel — designed to sit next to the reader as a quiet, beautiful companion to whatever passage you're studying.

## Today's Hebrew date

At the top, a date strip shows the **approximate** Hebrew date in Hebrew script (using the Cardo serif), its transliteration, and the year (e.g. 5786). The date is prefixed with **≈** because CODEX uses a simple anchor-based approximation for the day-of for this readout — expect up to a day or two of drift, especially around the year boundary at Tishrei 1.

## This week's parsha

The central card shows **Parashat HaShavua** — the weekly Torah portion. You'll see the Hebrew name in large serif type, its transliteration, the English meaning, the Torah portion range, and the traditional haftarah reading. **Every reference is clickable** and will jump the reader to that passage. Use the **PREV / NEXT / JUMP TO TODAY** pills to walk the 54-parsha annual cycle without changing what you have open.

## Holiday awareness

If a major holiday is within 14 days, a **gold-trimmed card** appears with the Hebrew name, English transliteration, date, and clickable scripture readings. Otherwise a quiet line tells you which holiday is next and how far out it is. Holiday cards show the **exact Gregorian civil date** for the current (or any) year — computed offline, no network required.

## Calendar reference

Two collapsible sections let you browse the full **12 Hebrew months** (with notes) and the **complete list of major holidays** (Rosh HaShanah through Tisha B'Av) with all their readings and their exact Gregorian dates.

## Daf Yomi

When the Daf Yomi reading-plan module is available, today's daf is shown as a chip you can copy or paste into an external Talmud lookup. (CODEX does not yet ship Talmud text — Sefaria deep-links are planned.)

articles.title: Public Data API (v1)
articles.body:
# CODEX Data API (v1)

CODEX exposes a small, read-only **public REST API** over its bundled study modules. Build dashboards, browser extensions, Alfred workflows, hardware displays, or research scripts against it. All endpoints are `GET`, return JSON, and are CORS-open.

**Base path:** `/api/v1`

## Endpoints

- `GET /api/v1/health-public` — `{ ok, version, modules_count }`. Safe for monitoring; reveals no key or usage info.
- `GET /api/v1/modules` — every bundled module's metadata in one shot.
- `GET /api/v1/strongs/:id` — Strong's lexicon entry. `H<n>` Hebrew or `G<n>` Greek.
- `GET /api/v1/crossref/:ref` — TSK cross-references for a verse (e.g. `gen.1.1`, `john.3.16`).
- `GET /api/v1/timeline?from=-2000&to=2000&category=narrative&era=patriarchs` — filter the biblical timeline.
- `GET /api/v1/parsha?week=current` — current or specified ISO-week parsha.
- `GET /api/v1/search?q=love&translation=kjv` — **stub**. Server-side full-text search is planned for Phase 5.6; CODEX currently searches client-side. The endpoint returns an explanatory `note`.

## Rate limits

100 requests per minute per IP. Every response sets `X-RateLimit-Limit` and `X-RateLimit-Remaining`; exceeding returns `429` with a `Retry-After` header.

## Example

```bash
curl http://localhost:3000/api/v1/strongs/H1
curl http://localhost:3000/api/v1/crossref/gen.1.1
curl 'http://localhost:3000/api/v1/timeline?from=-100&to=100'
```

## What's mock vs real

Real endpoints back the lexicon, cross-references, timeline, parsha, and module enumeration directly from the bundled JSON. `search` is a **documented stub** until the full-text index ships.

See [`API.md`](../../API.md) for the complete reference, error shapes, and response examples.

articles.title: Word Study (Λ)
articles.body:
# Word Study Λ

The **WORD** panel (Λ) is a deep-dive on a single Greek, Hebrew, or English word — the lexicon entry, where it lives in scripture, and the theological weight it carries.

## Triggering a study

- **Click any word** in the reader — if it has Strong's data wired up, the panel opens to that lemma.
- **From the verse menu**, choose **Word Study** to pivot from a verse you're reading.
- **From the Strong's panel**, opening a Strong's number broadcasts to Word Study too, so both panels stay in sync.
- **Search box** at the top of the panel — type a word (`love`) or a Strong's number (`G25`, `H157`).

## What you see

1. **Hero** — the original-language word in large Cardo serif, transliteration, Strong's number, and gloss.
2. **Semantic range** — bullet points parsed from the lexicon definition.
3. **Frequency** — a horizontal bar chart by book, computed from your own library via `CODEX_SEARCH`. The more chapters you've opened, the richer this becomes.
4. **First occurrences** — five clickable verses, snippet included; tap to jump straight to the reader.
5. **Related words** — an AI lexicographer call returns related Greek, Hebrew counterparts, and antonyms with their Strong's numbers. Cached per word for 30 days; only fires when an AI engine is configured in Settings.
6. **Why it matters** — a one-paragraph theological synthesis in a serif pull-quote. Also cached.
7. **Key passages** — surfaced for high-frequency lemmas.

The last studied word is remembered between sessions.

articles.title: Module Marketplace (⌬)
articles.body:
# Module Marketplace ⌬

The **MARKET** panel (⌬) is CODEX's storefront for **community-contributed study modules** — lexicons, cross-reference databases, commentaries, reading plans, dictionaries, atlases, language packs, and more.

## How modules work

A *module* is a plain JSON file with a `meta` envelope (`id`, `type`, `version`) plus typed payload. The runtime — `modules.js` — fetches, validates, and caches modules in IndexedDB, so once installed they work offline. The full envelope and every supported `type` are documented in `SPEC.md`.

## Sections in the panel

- **Installed** — every module currently in your IndexedDB cache. Each row shows type, version, and install date, with a **Remove** button.
- **Featured** — a curated grid of the most useful community modules.
- **Browse by category** — filter chips for Lexicons, Cross-Refs, Commentaries, Reading Plans, Dictionaries, Maps, Timelines, Language Packs, and Devotionals.
- **Add by URL** — paste any URL pointing to a valid module JSON, supply the expected module id, hit **Install**. The runtime validates the `meta` envelope and refuses anything malformed.
- **Add by file** — drag-drop a `.json` module onto the dropzone (or click to pick) to install from disk. Great for previewing a module you're authoring before publishing.

## Module detail view

Click any card or row to open a full detail page: long description, source link, license, install or remove button, and a collapsible **Raw JSON** preview.

## Curated index & contributions

The Featured and Browse lists are sourced from `data/module-index.json` — a curated catalog that ships with the app. Entries marked **COMING SOON** are placeholders describing planned modules and aren't yet hosted; they're shown so you can see what's on the roadmap. To contribute a new module to the curated index, see [`CONTRIBUTING.md`](../../CONTRIBUTING.md) in the CODEX repo.

articles.title: How CODEX Compares (⚖)
articles.body:
# How CODEX Compares ⚖

The **COMPARE** panel (⚖) shows a side-by-side, honest assessment of CODEX against the seven most-used Bible study apps: **Logos**, **e-Sword**, **YouVersion**, **Sefaria**, **Olive Tree**, and **Blue Letter Bible**.

## What's in the panel

1. **Hero stat strip** — the four numbers that matter most: price ($0), offline (100%), license (open source), AI-native (yes).
2. **Feature matrix** — 29 features × 7 apps. Cells are colored ✓ accent, ⚠ amber, ✗ dim, or `$$$` for paywalled. Hover any cell for a sourcing note.
3. **Price chart** — horizontal bars scaled to a $5,000 ceiling. Logos stretches across; CODEX is a single `$0` pill.
4. **Coverage radar** — an 8-axis SVG radar (Original Languages, AI Features, Offline, Reading Experience, Study Depth, Mobile, Customization, Community). Click legend chips to toggle apps.
5. **Love / Frustration cards** — aggregate sentiment per competitor, pulled from Reddit, App Store reviews, and blog posts (2024–26).
6. **Roadmap** — the gaps we know about: full Strong's, full TSK, Capacitor native apps, Linux Tauri, more translations.

## Honesty policy

CODEX is younger than every competitor on this list. Some rows we lose — **translations count** (YouVersion has 2,800+, we have 43+), **mobile native** (Capacitor builds are still cooking). Those losses are visible in the matrix and called out in the roadmap.

## SHARE THIS

The button at the bottom copies the full feature matrix as a Markdown table to your clipboard. Paste it into Reddit, Discord, or a tweet.

## Found a wrong row?

The entire dataset lives in `compare.jsx`. Open it, fix it, and send a PR via [`CONTRIBUTING.md`](../../CONTRIBUTING.md). Sourcing this honestly is a community job.

articles.title: VOX — Voice & Prayer (◉)
articles.body:
# VOX — Voice & Prayer ◉

The **VOX** panel (◉) turns CODEX into a reading companion that can speak any passage aloud in dozens of languages — and a prayer guide that holds reverent space across more than twenty traditions.

Every tradition is welcome here. Christian, Catholic, Orthodox, Jewish, Messianic, Sufi, Gnostic, Hermetic, Quaker, New Age, interfaith — all valid prayer formats.

## Reading mode

1. **Source** — read the current chapter, just the current verse, or paste any custom text (any language).
2. **Voice** — VOX auto-picks a voice matching your active translation's language. The dropdown groups every system voice by language; voices marked **★** are Natural / Neural / Enhanced / Premium quality (Apple voices on macOS & iOS, Google WaveNet on Android, etc.).
3. **Speed, pitch, volume** — sliders save per-voice automatically.
4. **Play / Pause / Stop** — large 44px touch targets. **VERSE ◀ / ▶** jumps the reader by one verse.
5. **Live highlight** — as TTS speaks, VOX dispatches `codex:vox-progress` with the current verse reference so the reader (or any panel) can highlight along.
6. **Read aloud from any verse** — open the verse menu on any verse and choose **Read aloud** to start playback from that verse without touching the panel.

### Voice quality

Native `speechSynthesis` is zero-cost, offline-capable, and ships with the OS. Quality varies:

- **macOS / iOS** — Apple's Natural / Enhanced / Premium voices (English, Spanish, French, German, Italian, Hebrew, Arabic, Chinese, and more).
- **Chrome / Edge on Windows** — Microsoft Neural voices when installed.
- **Android** — Google TTS, including high-quality WaveNet voices.
- **Linux** — typically eSpeak (robotic but functional).

The **✦ ENHANCE VOICE** button is reserved for a future cloud-TTS upgrade (OpenAI / ElevenLabs / etc.) via a server endpoint.

## Prayer mode

Switch to **✶ PRAYER** to browse 20+ prayer formats, each rendered as a tradition-tinted card. Pick one and CODEX shows you the prayer in big serif type with structured sections (invocation / petition / declaration / refrain / silence / doxology). Tap **✶ GUIDE ME** and the voice engine reads it aloud at a contemplative pace — pausing between sections, holding true silence for the duration each silence-section specifies.

The shipped traditions include: **The Lord's Prayer**, **Lectio Divina**, **The Examen**, **Breath Prayer / Jesus Prayer**, **Rosary — Joyful Mysteries**, **Compline (Night Prayer)**, **Trisagion**, **Akathist — Glory to God for All Things**, **Shema Yisrael**, **Amidah (Standing Prayer)**, **Birkat HaMazon**, **Modeh Ani**, **Al-Fatiha** (for interfaith study), **Hesychast Prayer Rope**, **Sufi Dhikr**, **Thomasine Reflection** (Gospel of Thomas), **As Above, So Below** (Hermetic), **Land Acknowledgment & Thanksgiving**, **Quaker Silence**, **Shema with Yeshua** (Messianic), **Invocation of the Inner Light** (New Age contemplative), and a **Free-form Petitionary** slot for AI-composed prayer.

### Generate a personalized prayer

Open the **Free-form Petitionary** card. Pick a tradition chip (Christian, Catholic, Orthodox, Jewish, Messianic, Sufi, Gnostic, Hermetic, Quaker, New Age, Interfaith), describe your situation — *anxiety before a job interview, grief over a friend, gratitude after recovery* — and press **✦ COMPOSE**. CODEX calls the configured AI engine to draft a reverent ~150-word prayer in that tradition's conventions. **▶ READ ALOUD** then reads it back at a contemplative pace.

### Prayer timer

For fixed-length practices — five-minute lectio, twenty-minute centering prayer — tap a timer chip. A gentle *Amen* is spoken when the timer ends.

articles.title: Continuity & Mastery (The Analyst Desk)
articles.body:
# Continuity & Mastery — The Analyst Desk

CODEX tracks the *shape* of your study, not just the days you showed up. Two instruments do this: **Continuity** (a forgiving reading streak) and **Mastery** (depth accrued across the canon). Open the **Analyst Dossier** — the continuity readout, glyph ◆ — to see the whole desk at a glance.

## Not the reading-plan streak

This is **not** the same counter as a reading-plan streak. A reading plan tracks one assigned schedule; miss a day and that plan's flame can break. **Continuity is different by design** — it is grace-shaped and depth-gated, with no guilt and no broken flame.

## Grace-shaped, depth-gated

Continuity advances **only on real depth actions** — completing a quest step, writing a note, closing a thread. Merely opening panels does nothing. And a single missed day will **not** snap your streak: Continuity quietly spends a **grace token** to cover the gap.

### Grace tokens

- You can hold a maximum of **2** grace tokens.
- You earn **+1 token every 7 days** of activity.
- A token is **spent silently** to bridge a missed day — no prompt, no penalty.

The Dossier shows how much **grace** is currently held.

## The eight mastery domains

Mastery accrues by **depth, not by clicks**. Eight domain bars in the Dossier chart where your study has gone deep — each fills as you do substantive work in that area. Curated quests (read / find / connect / reflect) are the main fuel.

## What the Dossier shows

- **Grace held** — your current grace-token balance.
- **The eight mastery-domain bars.**
- **Books at depth** — how many books you've genuinely worked, not merely opened.
- **Longest thread** — your deepest sustained line of study.
- **Current season** — the present chapter of your study arc.
- **Next-thread suggestion** — a nudge toward where to study next.
- **INTEL LOG** — a running record of your depth actions and milestones.

## Quests & the quest player

Curated quests come as four move types — **read**, **find**, **connect**, **reflect** — and feed mastery as you close them. The quest player walks you through each step. When a quest closes it **exports as a `.codex-study` file** you can keep, re-import, or share.

## A note on streaks

Continuity is meant to *hold* you, not pressure you. A quiet week costs at most a grace token; a lapse beyond your tokens simply resets the count without fanfare. Keep reading at depth and it climbs again.

articles.title: The Agentic OS
articles.body:
# The Agentic OS

Under the reader, CODEX runs like a small operating system — one door in, a kernel that can use the app's own tools, windows you arrange yourself, and a thread that remembers where you've walked. Nothing here requires an account, and nothing leaves your device unless you ask it to.

## One door: the ⌘K omnibar

Press `Cmd/Ctrl+K` anywhere. The omnibar is the single entrance to everything:

- **Type a reference** like `rev 21:4` and the verse *previews inline* before you commit — you see where you're going before you jump.
- **Type a verb**: `sword John 1:1` opens that verse in the word-study drill. Verbs are commands; the omnibar reads them like a shell.
- **Just type words** and live search runs across scripture, your notes, and this wiki as you go.
- **Ask a question** — anything shaped like a question becomes a *mission* the kernel can run.
- **Type `weave`** to gather your recent trail into a study (see the Loom below).

One box, many doors behind it. You never have to remember where a feature lives — only what you want.

## ❖ Missions

When a question becomes a mission, the kernel doesn't free-associate. It calls the app's **own tools** — search, cross-references, Strong's, the timeline, the dictionary — and assembles what it finds into an answer with **cited artifacts**: every claim points back to a verse, an entry, or a tool result you can open yourself. Caveats are mandatory, not decorative. If the evidence is thin or contested, the mission says so in plain words. You are always one tap from the raw material.

## Windows, not pages

The depth consoles — word study, gematria, cross-references, compare, and friends — open as **windows** you can drag anywhere, snap to screen edges, or dock to chips. Build the desk you want: scripture in the center, two consoles parked at the side, everything else closed. Layouts are yours to arrange and yours to abandon.

## ⟐ The Loom

As you read, CODEX quietly keeps your **local trail** — the verses you visited, the words you drilled, the connections you followed. Type `weave` (or open the Loom from ⌘K) and that trail is woven into a study: a readable record of where your attention actually went, with the passages laid in order. The trail lives **on your device, never uploaded**. Clear it whenever you like; it belongs to you the way a notebook does.

## The honesty rules

The whole stack runs under two standing rules:

1. **Survey, not verdict.** When traditions disagree, CODEX lays the readings side by side and tells you who holds which view. It does not pick a winner for you.
2. **Computed, not asserted.** Numeric tools like gematria show their arithmetic — letter values, sums, the method used — and present results as *computations you can check*, never as hidden meanings declared from on high.

An assistant that names its sources, shows its math, and keeps your trail at home is the only kind worth letting near scripture.

> "For now we see through a glass, darkly; but then face to face: now I know in part; but then shall I know even as also I am known." — *1 Corinthians 13:12*
